name: Around the World in 8 Bites.
description:
Did you know Fanta existed because of the 2nd world war? Or that the iconic Paella from Spain started out as a peasant food?

Join our Experimentor Vinaya on a flavorful journey tracing iconic food born from moments around the globe, be it wars, famines, migrations, empires, and revolutions. Each stop pairs a resilient recipe or ingredient with a Calorie exhibit, revealing how they shaped everyday eating, and its hidden science, politics, and survival stories.

name: Exhibition Opening of 'IMAGINE: Coral Reef'
description:
Join us for the opening of ‘IMAGINE Coral Reef.’ The exhibition is a compelling exploration of the beauty and fragility of coral ecosystems, inviting audiences to rethink design through the lens of regeneration. Inspired by coral reefs, often called the “rainforests of the sea”, the exhibition highlights the urgency of protecting our oceans and marine life, while showcasing innovative approaches to ecological responsibility. ​

The exhibition is developed in Vienna, Austria, by Regenerative Design and curated by the Institute of Design Research Vienna (IDRV). It is supported by the Austrian Federal Ministry for European and International Affairs, brought to India by the Austrian Cultural Forum New Delhi.

The event will feature opening remarks and an introduction to the exhibition.

name: Yele Oota
description:
When did nourishment become a commodity?

Explore this idea through the lens of the contemporary dance piece ‘Yele Oota’ performed by the Vishwakiran Nambi Dance Company. The performance explores humanity's most fundamental relationship, our connection with food and the act of sharing nourishment.

Through seven dancers and movement narratives, the choreography shifts between depicting resource accumulation and the gentle art of giving. Drawing from traditions where feeding others was considered the highest service, the piece celebrates the timeless wisdom of community kitchens and shared meals.

Using edible props that transform throughout the performance, ‘Yele Oota’ builds toward a powerful revelation: generosity is not merely a choice but a survival strategy for human civilisation.

name: Mise en Play: Play as a Creative Tool
description:
Every day, without thinking about it, you make sense of your surroundings through what you eat, what you hear, what your body tells you. 

We are all meaning-making creatures, and our earliest instinct was to do this through play. Play is a creative act in itself. Growing up, play was how we explored, expressed, and understood the world. Somewhere along the way, we stopped. Here is an invitation to start again.

This programme takes you through CALORIE as a space where many different perspectives and processes come together, and in doing so, we begin to see creativity not as a skill but as a practice. That practice is what we invite you into.

Expect hands-on activities that engage all your senses. You need no background in art or design, only an openness to notice, engage, and explore. Leave with a new way of observing your environment and documenting your experiences.

Join experimentors Nirupama and Shreya for a programme that treats creativity as something that belongs to everyone.

name: Whose Market Is This?
description:
‘Whose Market Is This?’ invites Bengaluru’s citizens to reflect on the food markets that feed their city. 

Across Bengaluru, food markets are evolving, shrinking, or disappearing entirely. In this context, engaging with and documenting market spaces becomes increasingly important.

Food systems researcher and storyteller Shivani Unakar, and photographer and food stylist Sanskriti Bist, will guide participants through Bengaluru’s century-old Russell market, sharing tools and approaches to documenting markets on the ground.

Join this workshop to learn where the produce you eat comes from, who keeps the city’s food systems alive, and why preserving these spaces matters.

This walk explores the themes of ‘BUY’ in relation to Science Gallery Bengaluru’s Exhibition CALORIE, the breakdown.

name: What Knowledge Does A Market Possess?
description:
Across India, markets are increasingly being “modernised”—given facelifts and reorganised into more sanitised, formal spaces. However, stories of redevelopment are increasingly revealing how livelihoods are disrupted and histories erased.

Oishika Roy, Features Editor at The Locavore, will explore how documenting markets offers a deeper understanding of the food system: how people eat, what shapes our choices, and who sustains them. Drawing from The Locavore’s Market Archives series, Oishika explores markets beyond solitary sites of transactions, as lived experiences and knowledge. 

In the context of a rapidly changing food landscape—with market spaces disappearing or being irrevocably altered—we ask, what forms of local knowledge and food cultures are also at risk?

This talk explores the themes of ‘BUY’ in relation to Science Gallery Bengaluru’s Exhibition CALORIE, and the breakdown.

name: ​We Are in a Soup
description:
When did you last cook together?

​We Are in a Soup is a participatory workshop that uses cooking and making as ways to return to presence, relation, and care. In a time shaped by speed, crisis, and emotional numbness, the workshop invites participants to slow down through simple, shared acts. Each participant brings a vegetable, and together they prepare a single pot of soup, chopping, stirring, and serving it collectively.

Alongside this, participants shape clay bowls by hand, creating vessels that hold touch, time, and memory. Cooking, serving, and cleaning are shared equally, shifting the focus from consumption to participation. Food becomes more than nourishment; it becomes a way of being together. This workshop creates a temporary space for warmth, attention, and collective presence, where care is practised rather than performed.

​This walk explores the themes of ‘Cook’ & ‘Eat’ in relation to Science Gallery Bengaluru’s Exhibition CALORIE, the breakdown.

name: The Great Indian Kitchen: Film Screening and Discussion
description:
How does everyday domestic labour, especially around food, become a site of control, invisibility, and resistance within patriarchal households? 

The Great Indian Kitchen, the opening film of the festival, explores the politics of gendered labour and the cultural meanings embedded in food practices. Through its intimate portrayal of a newly married woman’s life, the film reveals how routine kitchen work reflects larger structures of power and inequality. 

In conversation, Jeo Baby and Nilanjana Sengupta will unpack these themes, examining how cinema can foreground the often-unseen labour of women while questioning deeply rooted social norms around care, duty, and domesticity.

name: Colonies in Conflict: Film Screening and Discussion
description:
What would you do if a colony of wild honeybees made themselves your neighbours? 

Colonies in Conflict follows the migratory rock bee across Indian cities, forests, and farmlands, tracing the very different ways people understand and respond to their presence. For urban dwellers, the bees are a nuisance to be removed. For farmers, they are quiet partners in pollination, arriving and leaving with the seasons. Shot over two years across multiple locations in India, the film is the first to capture rock bees in close detail and, in doing so, surfaces what their declining numbers reveal about pollution, pesticide use, and the pressures of intensive agriculture.

In conversation after the screening, filmmaker Rajani Mani and ecologist Geetha G Thimmegowda, one of the scientists featured in the film, will discuss what it takes to tell a story at this scale, from a Bengaluru neighbourhood to the wider ecosystems that rock bees move through, and what it would actually take to make room for them.

name: Benda-Cal-ooru : Calorie meets Bengaluru
description:
What’s your favourite pastime in Bengaluru? Is it going to your local dharshini for thindi, or cafe hopping in the
evening? Or do you complain about the traffic and just order in? 

One thing is for sure, the city has much to offer for food. But if there’s so much consumption, what of the production? How does Bengaluru’s history and its institutions contribute to this culture? Come for an evening of play and exploration of Bengaluru’s history,institutions and their connection to food!
Join our Experimentors Megha and Tejashree for their walkthrough, to experience Bengaluru Kathe Calorie Jothe.
